question: A line passing through the point  $P(\sqrt{5}, \sqrt{5})$  intersects the ellipse  $ rac{x^2}{36} +  rac{y^2}{25} = 1$  at  $A$  and  $B$  such that  $(PA) \cdot (PB)$  is maximum. Then  $5(PA^2 + PB^2)$  is equal to :
explanation:
Given ellipse is  $\frac{x^2}{36} + \frac{y^2}{25} = 1$

![Diagram of an ellipse centered at the origin. A line segment AB passes through a point P(sqrt(5), sqrt(5)) on the ellipse. The line AB is horizontal, parallel to the x-axis, at y = sqrt(5).](https://tripple-score.sfo3.digitaloceanspaces.com/TripleScore/JEE_Mains_2025_Shift_1_Question_Paper_Apr_03_With_Solutions/b615ff07e8a0f467f0a6f4783c4463eb_img.jpg)

Diagram of an ellipse centered at the origin. A line segment AB passes through a point P(sqrt(5), sqrt(5)) on the ellipse. The line AB is horizontal, parallel to the x-axis, at y = sqrt(5).

Any point on line AB can be assumed as  $Q(\sqrt{5} + r \cos \theta, \sqrt{5} + r \sin \theta)$

Putting this in equation of ellipse, we get

$$25(\sqrt{5} + r \cos \theta)^2 + 36(\sqrt{5} + r \sin \theta)^2 = 900$$

Simplifying, we get

$$r^2(25 \cos^2 \theta + 36 \sin^2 \theta) + 2\sqrt{5}r(25 \cos \theta + 36 \sin \theta) - 595 = 0$$

$|r| = PA, PB$

$$\text{Thus, } PA \cdot PB = \frac{595}{25 \cos^2 \theta + 36 \sin^2 \theta} = \frac{595}{25 + 11 \sin^2 \theta}$$ 

$$= \text{maximum, if } \sin^2 \theta = 0$$ 

This means line AB must be parallel to x-axis

$$\Rightarrow y_A = y_B = \sqrt{5}$$ 

Putting  $y = \sqrt{5}$  in equation of ellipse, we get

$$\frac{x^2}{36} + \frac{1}{5} = 1 \Rightarrow x^2 = 36 \cdot \frac{4}{5}$$ 

Hence,

$$PA^2 + PB^2 = \left(\sqrt{5} - \frac{12}{\sqrt{5}}\right)^2 + \left(\sqrt{5} + \frac{12}{\sqrt{5}}\right)^2$$ 

$$= 2\left(5 + \frac{144}{5}\right) = \frac{338}{5}$$ 

$$5(PA^2 + PB^2) = 338$$

question: The sum  $1 + 3 + 11 + 25 + 45 + 71 + \dots$  upto 20 terms, is equal to
explanation:
Given sum is
$$S_n = 1 + 3 + 11 + 25 + 45 + 71 + \dots + T_n$$
First order differences are in A.P.
Thus, we can assume that
$$T_n = an^2 + bn + c$$
$$\text{Solving } \begin{cases} T_1 = 1 = a + b + c \ T_2 = 3 = 4a + 2b + c \ T_3 = 11 = 9a + 3b + c \end{cases},$$we get  $a = 3, b = -7, c = 5$
Hence, general term of given series is
$$T_n = 3n^2 - 7n + 5$$
Hence, required sum equals
$$\sum_{n=1}^{20} (3n^2 - 7n + 5) = 3\left(\frac{20 \cdot 21  41}{6}\right) - 7\left(\frac{20  21}{2}\right) + 5(20) = 7240$$

question:
If the domain of the function

$$f(x) = \log_e\left(\frac{2x-3}{5+4x}\right) + \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{4+3x}{2-x}\right) \text{ is } [\alpha, \beta],$$

then  $\alpha^2 + 4\beta$  is equal to
explanation:
Given function is

$$f(x) = \log_e\left(\frac{2x-3}{5+4x}\right) + \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{4+3x}{2-x}\right)$$

For domain, the conditions are

$$\frac{2x-3}{5+4x} > 0 \text{ and } \left|\frac{4+3x}{2-x}\right| \leq 1$$

$$\text{Now, } \frac{2x-3}{5+4x} > 0 \Rightarrow x \in \left(-\infty, -\frac{5}{4}\right) \cup \left[\frac{3}{2}, \infty\right)$$

$$\text{and } -1 \leq \frac{4+3x}{2-x} \leq 1$$

$$\Rightarrow \left(-1 \leq \frac{4+3x}{2-x}\right) \cap \left(\frac{4+3x}{2-x} \leq 1\right)$$

$$\Rightarrow \left(\frac{6+2x}{2-x} \geq 0\right) \cap \left(\frac{2+4x}{2-x} \leq 0\right)$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{6+2x}{2-x} \cdot \frac{2+4x}{2-x} \leq 0$$

$$\Rightarrow x \in \left[-3, -\frac{1}{2}\right]$$

Hence, we get the domain of f as  $x \in \left[-3, -\frac{5}{4}\right)$

This means that  $\alpha = -3, \beta = -\frac{5}{4}$

Thus,  $\alpha^2 + 4\beta = 9 - 5 = 4$
subject: Maths

question: If  $\sum_{r=1}^9 \left(\frac{r+3}{2^r}\right) \cdot {}^9C_r = \alpha \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^9 - \beta, \alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{N}$ , then  $(\alpha + \beta)^2$  is equal to
explanation:
Given that

$$\sum_{r=1}^9 \left(\frac{r+3}{2^r}\right) \cdot {}^9C_r = \alpha \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^9 - \beta, \alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{N}$$

Now,

$$\begin{aligned} \sum_{r=1}^9 \left(\frac{r+3}{2^r}\right) \cdot {}^9C_r &= \sum_{r=1}^9 \left(\frac{r}{2^r}\right) \cdot {}^9C_r + \sum_{r=1}^9 \left(\frac{3}{2^r}\right) \cdot {}^9C_r \\ &= \sum_{r=1}^9 \left(\frac{9}{2^r}\right) \cdot {}^8C_{r-1} + 3 \sum_{r=1}^9 {}^9C_r \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^r \left[ \text{Using } \frac{{}^9C_r}{{}^8C_{r-1}} = \frac{9}{r} \right] \\ &= \frac{9}{2} \sum_{r=1}^9 {}^8C_{r-1} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{r-1} + 3 \left( \sum_{r=0}^9 {}^9C_r \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^r \right) - 1 \\ &= \frac{9}{2} \left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right)^8 + 3 \left( \left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right)^9 - 1 \right) \\ &= \frac{9}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^8 + 3 \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^9 - 3 = 6 \cdot \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^9 - 3 \end{aligned}$$ 

Hence,  $\alpha = 6, \beta = 3$ 

Thus  $(\alpha + \beta)^2 = 81$

question: Let  $\\vec{a} = \\hat{i} + \\hat{j} + \\hat{k}$ ,  $\\vec{b} = 3\\hat{i} + 2\\hat{j} - \\hat{k}$ ,  $\\vec{c} = \\lambda\\hat{j} + \\mu\\hat{k}$  and  $\\hat{d}$  be a unit vector such that  $\\vec{a} \\times \\hat{d} = \\vec{b} \\times \\hat{d}$  and  $\\vec{c} \\cdot \\hat{d} = 1$ , If  $\\vec{c}$  is perpendicular to  $\\vec{a}$ , then  $|3\\lambda\\hat{d} + \\mu\\vec{c}|^2$  is equal to
explanation:
$$\\vec{a} \\times \\vec{d} - \\vec{b} \\times \\vec{d} = 0$$ 
$$(\\vec{a} - \\vec{b}) \\times \\vec{d} = 0$$ 
$$ \\vec{d} = t(\\vec{a} - \\vec{b})$$ 
$$ \\vec{d} = t(-2\\hat{i} - \\hat{j} + 2\\hat{k})$$ 
$$|\\vec{d}| = 1$$ 
$$|t| = \\frac{1}{3}$$ 
$$ \\vec{c} \\cdot \\vec{a} = 0$$ 
$$ \\lambda + \\mu = 0$$ 
$$ \\mu = -\\lambda$$ 
$$ \\vec{c} = \\lambda(\\hat{j} - \\hat{k}), \\quad |\\vec{c}|^2 = 2\\lambda^2$$ 
$$ \\vec{c} \\cdot \\hat{d} = 1$$ 
$$ t(-2, -1, 2) \\cdot \\lambda(0, 1, -1) = 1$$ 
$$ \\lambda t = \\frac{-1}{3} \\Rightarrow \\lambda^2 = 1$$ 
$$|3\\lambda\\hat{d} + \\mu\\vec{c}|^2 = 9\\lambda^2|\\hat{d}|^2 + \\mu^2|\\vec{c}|^2 + 6\\lambda\\mu(\\hat{d} \\cdot \\vec{c})$$ 
$$= 3\\lambda^2 + 2\\lambda^4$$ 
$$= 5$$

question: During the melting of a slab of ice at 273 K at atmospheric pressure :
options: Internal energy of ice-water system remains unchanged.
Positive work is done by the ice-water system on the atmosphere.
Internal energy of the ice-water system decreases.
Positive work is done on the ice-water system by the atmosphere.
correct_answer: Positive work is done on the ice-water system by the atmosphere.
explanation: Volume decreases during melting of ice so positive work is done on ice water system by atmosphere Heat absorbed by ice water so $\Delta Q$ is positive, work done by ice water system is negative Hence by first law of thermodynamics $\Delta U = \Delta Q + \Delta W = \text{Positive}$ So internal energy increases
subject: Physics

question:
Two blocks of masses  $m$  and  $M$ , ( $M > m$ ), are placed on a frictionless table as shown in figure. A massless spring with spring constant  $k$  is attached with the lower block. If the system is slightly displaced and released then

( $\mu$  = coefficient of friction between the two blocks)

![Diagram showing two blocks, m and M, on a frictionless table. Block m is on top of block M. A spring with constant k is attached to the left of block M and fixed to a wall. An arrow labeled mu points to the right from the top of block m, indicating friction.](https://tripple-score.sfo3.digitaloceanspaces.com/TripleScore/JEE_Mains_2025_Shift_1_Question_Paper_Apr_03_With_Solutions/bb6d33498937738ff5dac8d91c9ebaad_img.jpg)

Diagram showing two blocks, m and M, on a frictionless table. Block m is on top of block M. A spring with constant k is attached to the left of block M and fixed to a wall. An arrow labeled mu points to the right from the top of block m, indicating friction.

(A) The time period of small oscillation of the two blocks is  $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{(m+M)}{k}}$

(B) The acceleration of the blocks is  $a = \frac{kx}{M+m}$

( $x$  = displacement of the blocks from the mean position)

(C) The magnitude of the frictional force on the upper block is  $\frac{\mu m|x|}{M+m}$

(D) The maximum amplitude of the upper block, if it does not slip, is  $\frac{\mu(M+m)g}{k}$

(E) Maximum frictional force can be  $\mu(M+m)g$ .

Choose the **correct** answer from the options given below :
explanation:
(A) As both blocks moving together so

$$\text{Time period} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m}{K}}; \text{ where } m = M + m$$

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{M+m}{K}}$$

(B) Let block is displaced by  $x$  in (+ve) direction so force on block will be in (-ve) direction

$$F = -Kx$$

$$(M+m)a = -Kx$$

$$a = -\frac{Kx}{(M+m)}$$

(C) As upper block is moving due to friction thus

$$f = ma = \frac{mKx}{(M+m)}$$

(D) This option is like two block problem in friction for maximum amplitude, force on block is also maximum, for which both blocks are moving together.

![Diagram showing two blocks, m and M, on a frictionless table. Block m is on top of block M. A force KA is applied to the left of block M, indicated by an arrow pointing left.](https://tripple-score.sfo3.digitaloceanspaces.com/TripleScore/JEE_Mains_2025_Shift_1_Question_Paper_Apr_03_With_Solutions/62cadae47bbb10ec50a9799a3fe0482a_img.jpg)

Diagram showing two blocks, m and M, on a frictionless table. Block m is on top of block M. A force KA is applied to the left of block M, indicated by an arrow pointing left.

![Diagram showing a single block m with a force f applied to its left, indicated by an arrow pointing left.](https://tripple-score.sfo3.digitaloceanspaces.com/TripleScore/JEE_Mains_2025_Shift_1_Question_Paper_Apr_03_With_Solutions/1a4655ab13ebfd068a361d525ff_img.jpg)

Diagram showing a single block m with a force f applied to its left, indicated by an arrow pointing left.

$$KA = (M+m)a$$

$$a = \frac{KA}{(M+m)}$$

$$f = ma = \frac{mKA}{(M+m)}$$

$$f_{\max} = f_t = \mu mg$$

$$f = \mu mg$$

$$\frac{mKA}{(M+m)} = \mu mg$$

$$A = \frac{\mu(M+m)g}{K}$$

(E) Maximum friction can be  $\mu mg$  as force is acting between blocks & normal force here is  $mg$ .
subject: Physics

question:
A parallel plate capacitor is filled equally (half) with two dielectrics of dielectric constant $\epsilon_1$ and $\epsilon_2$, as shown in figures. The distance between the plates is $d$ and area of each plate is $A$. If capacitance in first configuration and second configuration are $C_1$ and $C_2$ respectively, then $ rac{C_1}{C_2}$ is :

First Configuration

![Diagram of a parallel plate capacitor in the first configuration. The plates are horizontal. The top half (thickness d/2) is filled with a dielectric of constant epsilon_2 (indicated by diagonal hatching). The bottom half (thickness d/2) is filled with a dielectric of constant epsilon_1 (indicated by dots). Electrical contacts are shown at the top and bottom centers of the plates.](https://tripple-score.sfo3.digitaloceanspaces.com/TripleScore/JEE_Mains_2025_Shift_1_Question_Paper_Apr_03_With_Solutions/b1379438553c86ab5c3f3135d82c70aa_img.jpg)

Second Configuration

![Diagram of a parallel plate capacitor in the second configuration. The plates are horizontal. The left half (area A/2) is filled with a dielectric of constant epsilon_1 (indicated by dots). The right half (area A/2) is filled with a dielectric of constant epsilon_2 (indicated by diagonal hatching). Electrical contacts are shown at the top and bottom centers of the plates.](https://tripple-score.sfo3.digitaloceanspaces.com/TripleScore/JEE_Mains_2025_Shift_1_Question_Paper_Apr_03_With_Solutions/e8efd7a0ae9c3c392f50c3c5cc0ed342_img.jpg)
explanation:
![Diagram for the first configuration. It shows two horizontal plates separated by a distance d. The top plate is labeled C and the bottom plate is labeled C'. The top half of the gap (thickness d/2) is filled with a dielectric of constant epsilon_2 (hatched). The bottom half (thickness d/2) is filled with a dielectric of constant epsilon_1 (dotted). Vertical double-headed arrows on the left indicate the thickness of each dielectric layer is d/2.](https://tripple-score.sfo3.digitaloceanspaces.com/TripleScore/JEE_Mains_2025_Shift_1_Question_Paper_Apr_03_With_Solutions/db171a68c3ecb9a44dbeddb84694442f_img.jpg)

First configuration

Area of plate is $A$ .
then
$$C = \frac{\epsilon_2 \epsilon_0 A}{d/2} = \frac{2\epsilon_2 \epsilon_0 A}{d}$$

$$C' = \frac{\epsilon_1 \epsilon_0 A}{d/2} = \frac{2\epsilon_1 \epsilon_0 A}{d}$$

$$\text{Let } C_0 = \frac{\epsilon_0 A}{d}$$

$$C = 2\epsilon_2 C_0$$

$$C' = 2\epsilon_1 C_0$$

C & C' are in series

$$C_1 = \frac{CC'}{C+C'} = \frac{4\epsilon_2\epsilon_1 C_0^2}{2C_0(\epsilon_2 + \epsilon_1)}$$

$$= \frac{2\epsilon_2\epsilon_1 C_0}{(\epsilon_2 + \epsilon_1)}$$

![Diagram of a capacitor with two dielectric regions. The total thickness is d. The left region has dielectric constant epsilon_2 and capacitance C'. The right region has dielectric constant epsilon_1 and capacitance C. A vertical line separates the two regions. Arrows point to each region with their respective labels.](https://tripple-score.sfo3.digitaloceanspaces.com/TripleScore/JEE_Mains_2025_Shift_1_Question_Paper_Apr_03_With_Solutions/ace13edeb79bdfa129ed84fbb4ac44e5_img.jpg)

Second configuration

$$\text{Here } C = \frac{\epsilon_1 \epsilon_0 A}{2d} = \frac{\epsilon_1 C_0}{2}$$

$$C' = \frac{\epsilon_2 C_0}{2}$$

C & C' are in parallel

$$C_2 = C' + C = (\epsilon_1 + \epsilon_2) \frac{C_0}{2}$$

$$\text{Thus } \frac{C_1}{C_2} = \frac{2\epsilon_2\epsilon_1 C_0}{(\epsilon_2 + \epsilon_1)} \times \frac{2}{(\epsilon_1 + \epsilon_2) C_0}$$

$$= \frac{4\epsilon_2\epsilon_1}{(\epsilon_2 + \epsilon_1)^2}$$
subject: Physics

A piston of mass  $M$  is hung from a massless spring whose restoring force law goes as  $F = -kx^3$ , where  $k$  is the spring constant of appropriate dimension. The piston separates the vertical chamber into two parts, where the bottom part is filled with  $n$  moles of an ideal gas. An external work is done on the gas isothermally (at a constant temperature  $T$ ) with the help of a heating filament (with negligible volume) mounted in lower part of the chamber, so that the piston goes up from a height  $L_0$  to  $L_1$ , the total energy delivered by the filament is (Assume spring to be in its natural length before heating)

A heating filament is shown in the bottom part of the chamber.](https://tripple-score.sfo3.digitaloceanspaces.com/TripleScore/JEE_Mains_2025_Shift_1_Question_Paper_Apr_03_With_Solutions/4a104be5f84f688417d8c222ec4ce4fa_img.jpg)

explanation:
Using WET

Total energy supplied = gravitational potential energy + spring potential energy + work done by gas

$$Mg(L_1 - L_0) + \int_{L_0}^{L_1} kx^3 dx + nRT \ln

$$\left[\frac{L_1 A}{L_0 A}\right] + W_{\text{ext}} = 0$$

$$\frac{K}{4}[x^4]_{L_0}^{L_1} + Mg(L_1 - L_0) + \int_{L_0}^{L_1} kx^3 dx + nRT \ln

$$\left[\frac{L_1}{L_0}\right] + W_{\text{ext}} = 0$$

$$\frac{k}{4}(L_1^4 - L_0^4) + Mg(L_1 - L_0) + nRT \ln

$$\left[\frac{L_1}{L_0}\right] + W_{\text{ext}} = 0$$

$$W_{\text{ext}} = \frac{k}{4}(L_1^4 - L_0^4) + Mg(L_1 - L_0) + nRT \ln\left[\frac{L_1}{L_0}\right]$$

question: A particle is released from height  $S$  above the surface of the earth. At certain height its kinetic energy is three times its potential energy. The height from the surface of the earth and the speed of the particle at that instant are respectively.
explanation:
$$V^2 = 0 + 2g(S - x)$$

$$V^2 = 2g(S - x)$$

At B, Potential energy =  $mgx$

$$mgx = 3 \times \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

$$gx = \frac{3}{2} \times 2g(S - x)$$

$$4x = S$$

$$x = \frac{S}{4}$$

$$\Rightarrow V = \sqrt{2g \times \frac{3S}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{3gS}{2}}$$

question:
Three identical spheres of mass  $m$ , are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of length  $a$ . When released, they interact only through gravitational force and collide after a time  $T = 4$  seconds. If the sides of the triangle are increased to length  $2a$  and also the masses of the spheres are made  $2m$ , then they will collide after 

 seconds.
explanation:
$$T \propto M^x [M^{-1} L^3 T^{-2}]^y [L]^z$$

$$T \propto M^{x-y} L^{3y+z} T^{-2y}$$

$$x - y = 0 \Rightarrow x = y$$

$$-2y = 1 \Rightarrow y = -\frac{1}{2}, x = -\frac{1}{2}$$

$$\Rightarrow 3y + z = 0$$

$$z = -3y = \frac{3}{2}$$

Hence

$$T \propto m^{-1/2} G^{-1/2} a^{3/2}$$

$$T \propto \left(\frac{a^3}{m}\right)^{1/2}$$

$$T = 4 \times \left(\frac{2^3}{2}\right)^{1/2} = 8s$$
subject: Physics

question:

**Statement II :** Compounds of group 15 elements in +3 oxidation states readily undergo disproportionation reactions.

options: Statement I is true but statement II is false
Both statement I and statement II are false
Statement I is false but statement II is true
Both statement I and statement II are true
correct_answer: Both statement I and statement II are false
explanation:
$\\ddot{\\text{N}}-\\ddot{\\text{N}}$ single bond is weaker than $\\ddot{\\text{P}}-\\ddot{\\text{P}}$ due to more $l_p-l_p$ repulsion.

Bond length $\Rightarrow d_{\\text{P-P}} > d_{\\text{N-N}}$ (size $\\uparrow$, B.L. $\\uparrow$)

In group 15 elements only N & P do not show disproportionation in +3 oxidation state, As, Sb & Bi have almost inert for disproportionation in +3 oxidation state.

So both statements are false.

explanation: On addition of inert gas at constant P & T, reaction moves in the direction of greater no. of moles so it will shift in forward direction, so  $[	ext{PCl}_5]$  decrease and  $[	ext{PCl}_3]$  &  $[	ext{Cl}_2]$  will increase.
subject: Chemistry

Compound

question:
Given :

$$\Delta H_{\text{sub}}^\circ [\text{C}(\text{graphite})] = 710 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$ 

$$\Delta_{\text{C-H}} H^\circ = 414 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$ 

$$\Delta_{\text{H-H}} H^\circ = 436 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$ 

$$\Delta_{\text{C=C}} H^\circ = 611 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$ 

The  $\Delta H_f^\circ$  for  $\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2$  is 

$$\text{\_
atext{\_
atext{}}}\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$  (nearest integer value) 

![Diagram representing the enthalpy change for the formation of ethene](https://tripple-score.sfo3.digitaloceanspaces.com/TripleScore/JEE_Mains_2025_Shift_1_Question_Paper_Apr_03_With_Solutions/5a515f5f65583e486406aff3dd71e876_img.jpg)
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ccc} 2\text{C}_{(\text{s})} + 2\text{H}_{2(\text{g})} & \longrightarrow & \text{CH}_2 = \text{CH}_{2(\text{g})} : [\Delta H_f^\circ]_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_{4(\text{g})}} \\ 
 ↓ & ↖ & \\ 2\text{C}_{(\text{g})} + 4\text{H}_{(\text{g})} & & \end{array}$$ 

$$[\Delta H_f^\circ]_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_{4(\text{g})}} = 2 \times [\Delta H_{\text{sub}}^\circ]_{\text{C}_{(\text{s})}} + 2 \times \Delta H_{\text{H-H}}^\circ - 1 \times \Delta H_{\text{C=C}}^\circ - 4 \times \Delta H_{\text{C-H}}^\circ$$ 

$$\Rightarrow [\Delta H_f^\circ]_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_{4(\text{g})}} = (2 \times 710) + (2 \times 436) - 611 - 4 \times 414$$ 

$$\Rightarrow [\Delta H_f^\circ]_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_{4(\text{g})}} = 25 \text{ kJ / mol}}$$
subject: Chemistry
